content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 13 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_013_page_001.jpg

1. This is the third time I am coming to you. "Every charge must be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses."

2. I warned those who sinned before and all the others, and I warn them now while absent, as I did when present on my second visit, that if I come again I will not spare them —

3. since you seek proof that Christ is speaking in me. He is not weak in dealing with you, but is powerful among you.

4. For he was crucified in weakness, but lives by the power of God. For we also are weak in him, but in dealing with you we will live with him by the power of God.

5. Examine yourselves, to see whether you are in the faith. Test yourselves. Or do you not realize this about yourselves, that Jesus Christ is in you? — unless indeed you fail to meet the test!

6. I hope you will find out that we have not failed the test.

7. But we pray to God that you may not do wrong — not that we may appear to have met the test, but that you may do what is right, though we may seem to have failed.

8. For we cannot do anything against the truth, but only for the truth.

9. For we are glad when we are weak and you are strong. Your restoration is what we pray for.

10. For this reason I write these things while I am away from you, so that when I come I may not have to be severe in my use of the authority that the Lord has given me for building up and not for tearing down.

11. Finally, brothers, rejoice. Aim for restoration, comfort one another, agree with one another, live in peace; and the God of love and peace will be with you.

12. Greet one another with a holy kiss.

13. All the saints greet you.

14. The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 13 closes the letter with final warnings, the rhetorical reversal of the test, and the trinitarian benediction that has shaped Christian liturgy for two millennia.

**Verse 4 — The christological paradigm:** All three translators captured the crucifixion/resurrection pattern. SOPHIA affirms: this verse is the christological key that unlocks the entire letter — weakness and power are not opposites but sequential moments in the divine pattern.

**Verse 14 — The trinitarian benediction:** All three translators preserved the triadic structure. SOPHIA affirms: this is Paul's most explicitly triadic statement, and its liturgical legacy is incalculable.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "Every word will be established" vs. "Every charge must be established" vs. "The facts of every case"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "By [the] mouth of two witnesses and three every word will be established" | rhema as "word"; literal Deut 19:15 |
| LOGOS | "Every charge must be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses" | rhema as "charge"; legal context |
| PNEUMA | "The facts of every case must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses" | "Facts of every case" — legal expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. In the legal context of Deut 19:15, rhema functions as "charge/matter" — not simply "word." PNEUMA's "facts of every case" adds specificity beyond the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. rhema as "charge" in legal context.

---

### v.4 — "crucified from weakness" vs. "crucified in weakness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he was crucified from weakness" | ex astheneias — "from/out of weakness" |
| LOGOS | "he was crucified in weakness" | "In" — attendant circumstance |
| PNEUMA | "he was crucified in weakness" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The preposition ex ("from/out of") could mean "because of weakness" or "in a state of weakness." "In weakness" is the more natural English rendering and preserves the essential point: Christ's crucifixion was a genuine act of weakness.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Prepositional nuance — ex as "in" vs. "from."

---

### v.5 — "Christos is in you" vs. "Christ is in you" vs. "Christ is among you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Yeshua Christos is in you" | en hymin — "in you" (plural) |
| LOGOS | "Jesus Christ is in you" | Same — indwelling |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus Christ is among you" | "Among" — communal presence rather than individual indwelling |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. en hymin can mean either "in you" (indwelling) or "among you" (communal presence). Both are grammatically possible. LOGOS's "in you" preserves the ambiguity better — "among you" limits to communal presence and excludes individual indwelling.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. en hymin — "in you" preserves the ambiguity better than "among you."

---

### v.5 — "unapproved" vs. "fail to meet the test" vs. "failed the test of genuine faith"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unless you are unapproved" | adokimoi — "not approved/failing the test" |
| LOGOS | "unless indeed you fail to meet the test!" | Verbal rendering of adokimos |
| PNEUMA | "if not, you have failed the test of genuine faith" | Adds "of genuine faith" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. adokimos means "failing the test/not approved." PNEUMA adds "of genuine faith" — interpretive; Paul says simply "the test," leaving the criteria open.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA specifies what Paul leaves general.

---

### v.7 — Complex sentence: "we may seem to have failed" — structural handling

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not that we may appear approved, but that you may do the good, and we may be as unapproved" | Literal; three clauses |
| LOGOS | "not that we may appear to have met the test, but that you may do what is right, though we may seem to have failed" | Formal; concessive "though" |
| PNEUMA | "I hope we won't need to demonstrate our authority when we arrive. Do the right thing before we come — even if that makes it look like we have failed to demonstrate our authority" | Full restructuring; imperative added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul's point is subtle: he'd rather appear to have "failed" (because the Corinthians do right voluntarily) than appear to have "passed" (by exercising punitive authority). PNEUMA's restructuring, while capturing the gist, adds "demonstrate our authority" twice (not in Greek) and changes the rhetorical mode from prayer to imperative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes prayer to command.

---

### v.9 — "restoration" vs. "restoration" vs. "become mature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the restoration of you" | katartisin as "restoration" |
| LOGOS | "Your restoration is what we pray for" | Same term |
| PNEUMA | "We pray that you will become mature" | "Mature" for katartisis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. katartisis means "restoration/mending/fitting together" — not "maturity" (which would be teleiotis). The word evokes repairing what is broken, not developing what is immature. PNEUMA imports a different concept.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. katartisis ("restoration/mending") vs. "maturity" — Paul is praying for repair, not growth. The Corinthians are broken, not immature.

---

### v.11 — Five imperatives: rendering and structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "rejoice, be restored, be encouraged, be of one mind, be at peace" | Five imperatives; parakaleo as "be encouraged" |
| LOGOS | "rejoice. Aim for restoration, comfort one another, agree with one another, live in peace" | Five imperatives; katartizesthe as "aim for restoration" |
| PNEUMA | "Be joyful. Grow to maturity. Encourage each other. Live in harmony and peace" | Four imperatives (combines last two); "maturity" again |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The five imperatives should be preserved (PNEUMA combines two into one). PNEUMA again renders katartizesthe as "grow to maturity" rather than "aim for restoration" — same issue as v.9.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA merges imperatives and repeats the katartizo mistranslation.

---

### v.12 — "holy kiss" vs. "holy kiss"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Greet one another with a holy kiss" | Standard |
| LOGOS | "Greet one another with a holy kiss" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Greet each other with a holy kiss" | "Each other" for allelous |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Minimal difference — "one another" and "each other" are synonymous in modern English.

**Status:** Resolved. Trivial variant.

---

### v.13 — "holy ones" vs. "saints" vs. "God's people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "All the holy [ones] greet you" | hagioi as "holy ones" |
| LOGOS | "All the saints greet you" | "Saints" — standard |
| PNEUMA | "All of God's people here send you their greetings" | "God's people"; adds "here" and "send...their" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Saints" is the standard rendering of hagioi. PNEUMA adds "here" (not in Greek) and restructures the greeting.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds location detail.

---

### v.14 — The benediction: structural agreement, minor differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The grace of the Lord Yeshua Christos and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit [be] with all of you" | Transliterated names; supplies "[be]" |
| LOGOS | "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all" | Standard; "be with you all" |
| PNEUMA | "May the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all" | Adds "May" — optative mood explicit |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "May" makes the optative mood explicit, which is a reasonable English convention. However, the Greek has no verb — the "[be]" is supplied. LOGOS's simple "be" preserves the elliptical quality.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — "May...be" vs. "...be."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 3 | "Of genuine faith," "here," "demonstrate our authority" |
| PNEUMA katartizo mistranslation | 2 | "Maturity/mature" for "restoration" — vv.9, 11 |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 2 | Merges imperatives (v.11), changes prayer to command (v.7) |
| en hymin ambiguity | 1 | "In you" vs. "among you" (v.5) |
| rhema in legal context | 1 | "Word" vs. "charge" (v.1) |

**Overall pattern:** The final chapter's disagreements center on PNEUMA's rendering of katartisis/katartizo as "maturity/mature" rather than "restoration/mending" — a significant theological substitution that appears twice (vv.9, 11). Paul's prayer is for a broken community to be repaired, not for an immature community to grow up. The consensus preserves the mending imagery and the five-imperative structure of the farewell.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
